title: The Wall

jokes.text: Dear God, woman.
jokes.setup: Leslie enthusiastically launches into baby shower planning
jokes.punchline: Ron's horrified reaction to feminine celebration rituals
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ron Swanson character moment - his masculine discomfort with baby shower activities is exactly what we'd expect from this character

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Ron, I am so excited to plan the baby shower! We're going to have games, decorations, tiny little appetizers shaped like baby carriages...

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Leslie. No. Absolutely not. I will not participate in this feminine celebration ritual. Baby showers are a violation of everything I stand for.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Ron, it's just a party to celebrate the baby.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: It's a trap. A coordinated assault of pastel colors and forced sentimentality designed to extract emotions from grown men. I'd rather sit alone in a dark room eating breakfast food.

jokes.text: What are you doing up there in space, baby? Ah! So cute!
jokes.setup: Leslie planning to make astronaut onesie
jokes.punchline: Her immediate shift into baby voice and over-enthusiasm
jokes.explanation: Shows Leslie's tendency to get carried away with enthusiasm, but fairly standard cute overload humor

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I'm going to make an astronaut onesie. Oh my god, oh my god, oh my god! It's going to have a big helmet and little rocket boosters on the feet and—Ben, Ben, look at this! We're going to be the cutest astronauts in the whole universe! I'm going to bedazzle the helmet!

jokes.text: Oh, my God, whose baby is that? That would be mine.
jokes.setup: Leslie shocked to see a baby
jokes.punchline: Ron's deadpan revelation that it's his
jokes.explanation: Great setup/payoff with Ron's typical understated delivery of shocking news

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Oh my God, Ron, is that a baby?

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Yes.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Whose baby is that?

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Mine.

jokes.text: Guys, get in here! Ron has a baby!
jokes.setup: Ron quietly reveals he has a baby
jokes.punchline: Leslie immediately broadcasts it to everyone
jokes.explanation: Leslie's inability to keep private information private is very in character

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Ron has a baby! Ron has a baby! Ron has a baby!

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Leslie, I asked you not to—

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Everyone! Ron Swanson has a baby!

jokes.text: John middle name redacted Swanson
jokes.setup: Ron introducing his son formally
jokes.punchline: Treating baby introduction like classified information
jokes.explanation: Brilliant character-specific joke - Ron's paranoia and secrecy applied to baby introductions is perfectly absurd and quotable

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I'd like to introduce you all to my son. However, I must warn you that this information is classified. If I tell you his name, I will have to kill you.

jokes.text: John was born some time ago, weighing multiple pounds and several ounces
jokes.setup: Normal expectation of specific birth details
jokes.punchline: Ron providing deliberately vague, unhelpful information
jokes.explanation: Another excellent Ron moment - his refusal to share personal details becomes absurdly unhelpful

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I was born on a farm upstate. My parents were very private people. I don't really like to talk about my childhood.

jokes.text: Much like his father, he is a fan of silence. Please keep your voices down.
jokes.setup: Baby introduction continuing
jokes.punchline: Ron projecting his antisocial preferences onto newborn
jokes.explanation: Nice character consistency - Ron assuming his baby shares his antisocial tendencies

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I'm naming him Ramsett. He's antisocial and withdrawn. He hates people. He looks angry at the world.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Ron, he's a baby. He can't hate people.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Not yet. But he will. He'll learn. I'm going to teach him.

jokes.text: Why would you need to know something like that?
jokes.setup: Leslie asking why he didn't tell her about the baby
jokes.punchline: Ron genuinely confused why birth of child would be relevant information
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ron logic - genuinely baffled that others would want to know about his personal life

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Ron, why didn't you tell me about the baby?

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I didn't think it was relevant.

jokes.text: She's just walking around, wondering why I haven't sent her a gift yet.
jokes.setup: Leslie realizing she hasn't sent a gift
jokes.punchline: Her projection of social anxiety onto Diane
jokes.explanation: Shows Leslie's gift anxiety and tendency to project her concerns onto others

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Oh my God, I forgot to send Diane a wedding gift!

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: She probably thinks I don't care about her. She's probably telling everyone at work that I'm a terrible friend. Her mom is definitely disappointed in me. Diane's definitely disappointed. Her dog is disappointed.

jokes.text: Just let her tire herself out.
jokes.setup: Leslie spiraling about gift obligations
jokes.punchline: Ron treating her like an overexcited pet
jokes.explanation: Excellent economy and character work - Ron's practical approach to Leslie's hysteria

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Ron, I need to give gifts to everyone at the office. Do you know how many people that is? It's like forty people!

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Leslie, you're spiraling. Sit. Good girl. Here's a treat.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Ron, I'm not a dog.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: That's what the dog would say.

jokes.text: Could one of you please stop by the pet store and pick up some fish food for me? When did you get a fish?
jokes.setup: Ron asking for fish food
jokes.punchline: Revealing he has yet another secret pet/responsibility
jokes.explanation: Nice escalation of Ron's secretiveness - after revealing baby, another hidden responsibility

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I need to buy some fish food.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Fish food? Ron, do you have fish?

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Several.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Several? How many is several?

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I've stopped counting after twelve.

jokes.text: Um, he dislocates his shoulder to the music of Billy Joel. The Pawnee Journal called it, 'Why would anyone do this?'
jokes.setup: Suggesting Orin as headliner
jokes.punchline: Describing his bizarre performance art and newspaper's baffled review
jokes.explanation: Great absurdist humor with a perfectly quotable newspaper review that captures the bewilderment

dialogue.speaker: Leslie Knope
dialogue.line: Orin could be our headliner! He does experimental performance art.

dialogue.speaker: Ben Wyatt
dialogue.line: What kind of performance art?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie Knope
dialogue.line: Well, last week he spent three hours lying in a bathtub fully clothed, screaming about government bureaucracy while covered in mayonnaise.

dialogue.speaker: Ben Wyatt
dialogue.line: What did the newspaper say about it?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie Knope
dialogue.line: The review just said 'Why?'

jokes.text: It was going to be 15, but then this one guy described himself as 'Jackson Pollock-esque' and I told him that's finger painting for adults and I hate it!
jokes.setup: Craig reporting on art tent signups
jokes.punchline: His aggressive rejection of pretentious artist
jokes.explanation: Good Craig character moment - his intense reactions and art snobbishness coming through

dialogue.speaker: Craig
dialogue.line: We had a signup for the art tent. One guy wanted to display his work. I asked him what kind of art. He said, 'I create installations that challenge the bourgeois construct of reality.' So I told him we don't have room and he should leave.

jokes.text: Pawnee might once again tolerate-slash-ignore me
jokes.setup: Leslie explaining her goals for unity concert
jokes.punchline: Setting extremely low bar for political success
jokes.explanation: Good self-aware political humor - Leslie's realistic assessment of her standing

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I'm organizing a unity concert to bring the whole town together. My goal is to create an event so inclusive and non-partisan that literally no one can complain about it.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: If we can get through one evening without a single person tweeting something negative, I will consider this the greatest political achievement in the history of human civilization.

jokes.text: for a politician it's pretty much the best-case scenario
jokes.setup: Acknowledging that tolerance/ignoring sounds bad
jokes.punchline: Revealing how low political expectations really are
jokes.explanation: Solid political satire about low expectations for public officials

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Look, I know 'tolerance' sounds bad, but in politics, it's actually a huge compliment.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: It means, 'I don't think you're going to destroy everything.'

jokes.text: You guys might as well be a pile of leaves because you're about to get blown away.
jokes.setup: Tom building up dramatic announcement
jokes.punchline: Overly elaborate metaphor for being impressed
jokes.explanation: Typical Tom over-the-top metaphor, but fairly predictable

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: I'm about to say something that will blow your mind. Are you ready? Because this is big. This is really big. Like, career-defining big.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: You know how when you're driving down the highway at night, and suddenly you see this massive billboard lit up? And it's so bright and so unexpected that you have to shield your eyes? That's what you just did to my brain. You are that billboard. Except instead of advertising beer or car insurance, you're advertising pure excellence.

jokes.text: 'Grant Larson,' otherwise known as Elton John.
jokes.setup: Tom claiming Grant Larson is famous
jokes.punchline: Completely wrong celebrity comparison
jokes.explanation: Tom's tendency to make random celebrity comparisons that make no sense

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Grant Larson is basically the Ryan Gosling of municipal government.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Tom, Grant Larson looks nothing like Ryan Gosling.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Well, not anymore. Ryan Gosling's aged really well.

jokes.text: He's basically the Liam Bonneville of the Midwest. Liam Bonneville? The department of the interior's resident bad boy? He's basically the Tim Dweck-- Okay, never mind.
jokes.setup: Leslie trying to explain Grant's importance with comparisons
jokes.punchline: Each comparison getting more obscure until she gives up
jokes.explanation: Great escalating structure showing Leslie's wonky knowledge getting increasingly niche until even she realizes it's useless

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Ben is like... like the peanut butter to my jelly.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: No wait, he's like the Ross to my Rachel.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: He's like the Mulder to my Scully.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: He's like the Moonlight Sonata to my... Moonlight Concerto.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: You know what? He's just Ben. I can't explain it.

jokes.text: And that banner unfurling marks the 100th time that one of my projects has been... ahead of schedule.
jokes.setup: Leslie's dramatic banner moment
jokes.punchline: Arbitrary milestone celebrating being on time
jokes.explanation: Shows Leslie's need to celebrate everything and track weird statistics

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: We're here to celebrate an arbitrary milestone. Today marks the 47th consecutive day that I have arrived at work on time.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I'd like to thank my alarm clock, my car, and my unwavering commitment to municipal excellence.

jokes.text: besides the unveiling of Tricia's new highlights. You think I wouldn't notice? You look fly as hell, girl!
jokes.setup: Leslie calling unity concert biggest event of 2014
jokes.punchline: Immediately undermining it with praise for coworker's hair
jokes.explanation: Classic Leslie - getting distracted by supporting friends even during important speeches

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: This unity concert is going to be the biggest event of 2014.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: By the way, your hair looks amazing today.

jokes.text: I'm gonna do what I do best-- lock down deals. And dork-asaurus over here is gonna handle the boring stuff.
jokes.setup: Tom and Ben dividing responsibilities
jokes.punchline: Tom's made-up insult and role reversal
jokes.explanation: 'Dork-asaurus' is mildly creative but Tom calling business stuff boring when he's terrible at it is predictable

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Ben, you're being a real Donna right now.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: What does that even mean?

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: I don't know, but it's not a compliment. You're all up in my business about my responsibilities.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: That's literally my job. I'm the Deputy Director.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Well, I'm the Deputy Deputy Director of not caring what you think.

jokes.text: Also, I'm your boss.
jokes.setup: Ben pointing out Tom said he valued his business skills
jokes.punchline: Adding the more important fact that he's in charge
jokes.explanation: Good deadpan delivery and economy - Ben's practical response to Tom's flattery

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: You said you valued my business skills.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: But the more important fact is that I'm in charge.

jokes.text: It says 'Property of Pawnee government.' Isn't that adorable?
jokes.setup: Leslie showing baby gift
jokes.punchline: Treating bureaucratic labeling as cute baby wear
jokes.explanation: Perfect Leslie moment - her government obsession extending to inappropriate baby gifts

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Look at this adorable little onesie I got for the baby! It says 'Department of Parks and Recreation' on it.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I had it custom made. I wanted the baby to start learning about proper government organization from day one.

jokes.text: Today might be the day that I finally crowd surf. I should probably empty my pockets just in case.
jokes.setup: Leslie excited about press conference response
jokes.punchline: Preparing for crowd surfing at government announcement
jokes.explanation: Good character work - Leslie's unrealistic expectations for government event excitement

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I'm going to crowd surf at the press conference. Ben, help me practice by throwing me in the air repeatedly.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Leslie, no. That's not appropriate for a government announcement.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Ben, I've seen it work at concerts. How is this different?

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Because one involves music and a crowd that wants to be there, and the other involves city officials and a potential lawsuit.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: You're thinking about this all wrong. This is going to blow people's minds.

jokes.text: in front of the graffiti that says 'Pawnee, you suck,' or 'Go home, Eagleton snobs'?
jokes.setup: April asking where Leslie will stand
jokes.punchline: Revealing unity wall is covered in divisive graffiti
jokes.explanation: Good ironic contrast between unity message and hateful graffiti backdrop

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Where are you going to stand?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Right here by the unity wall.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Oh no.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: What?

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: You might want to look at it first.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Why would I— oh my God!

jokes.text: I painted over that graffiti a week ago. These people are the worst!
jokes.setup: Leslie's preparation efforts
jokes.punchline: Immediate return of graffiti and her exasperation
jokes.explanation: Shows the futility of Leslie's optimism against petty town divisions

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I spent all weekend removing graffiti from the park. All of it. Every single tag.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: That's admirable.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Thank you, Ron. I really think we can keep it clean if—

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Uh, Leslie? There's new graffiti on the bathroom door.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: What? I just— that was— NO!

jokes.text: I cannot announce a unity concert in front of a picture of a bald eagle giving the middle finger.
jokes.setup: Leslie's growing frustration with graffiti
jokes.punchline: Describing patriotic symbol corrupted into obscenity
jokes.explanation: Excellent absurd imagery - patriotic symbol turned vulgar perfectly captures town dysfunction

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Do you know what I saw today? Someone spray-painted over the American flag mural on Fifth and Main. They turned the eagle into... I can't even say it. It's an obscenity. A bald eagle! Our national bird! Desecrated!

jokes.text: You probably wanna stand to the side so people can see it.
jokes.setup: Leslie complaining about offensive eagle graffiti
jokes.punchline: April suggesting she highlight rather than hide it
jokes.explanation: Perfect April deadpan - subverting helpful advice to sabotage Leslie's event

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: This is unacceptable. Someone drew an offensive eagle on the community center wall.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Why don't you just highlight it? Make it a teaching moment. Add some flowers around it, maybe a inspirational quote.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: April, that's... actually that might work.

jokes.text: Ahh! Ow. The kool-aid guy makes it look so easy.
jokes.setup: Andy trying to break through wall like Leslie suggested
jokes.punchline: Failing and referencing Kool-Aid commercial character
jokes.explanation: Physical comedy with pop culture reference, but fairly predictable setup/payoff

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Oh yeah!

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Wait, that didn't work.

jokes.text: I've taken great pains to see as little of this building as possible. Turns out the third floor is silent, empty, and completely free of government work.
jokes.setup: Ron discovering new area of building
jokes.punchline: Finding paradise is place without government work
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ron philosophy - his ideal workspace is one where no government work happens

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Finding paradise is a place without government work.

jokes.text: You guys make my favorite teeth-whitening strips-- pearlies for girlies. It's unisex. The cartoon princess on the label says so.
jokes.setup: Tom praising sponsor's product
jokes.punchline: Product clearly marketed to girls but claiming it's unisex based on cartoon
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist logic about gendered marketing and Tom's vanity product knowledge

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: This product is completely unisex. Look, it comes in purple!

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: And the cartoon character on the box? Sure, she's got a bow in her hair and long eyelashes, but that doesn't mean it's just for girls.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Guys can wear bows. Guys can have eyelashes. It's 2014, people.

jokes.text: I may have a few eggs ready to hatch in the personal idea nest.
jokes.setup: Investor asking about Tom's business ideas
jokes.punchline: Overly elaborate metaphor for having ideas
jokes.explanation: Typical Tom over-complicated metaphor, fits character but isn't particularly clever

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: I've got ideas for days. I'm like a idea factory, except the factory is also an idea, and the ideas coming out of the factory are ideas about factories that make ideas.

jokes.text: Soon I'll have a new career, new crib, new friends. If all goes well, this may be one of the last times I ever have to speak to you.
jokes.setup: Tom celebrating potential business deal
jokes.punchline: Casually insulting Ben while asking for his help
jokes.explanation: Shows Tom's selfish enthusiasm but the joke structure is fairly predictable

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Ben, my man, I need your help with something. You're not smart, but you're like, organized-smart, you know? Like a smart person's filing cabinet.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: That's not a compliment, Tom.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: I know, but I need you anyway.

jokes.text: I was told there'd be a free barbecue buffet? Just take some beef jerky and...hush!
jokes.setup: Council member asking about promised food
jokes.punchline: Leslie's improvised inadequate solution and dismissal
jokes.explanation: Shows Leslie's event planning falling apart and her poor improvisation skills

dialogue.speaker: City Council Member
dialogue.line: Where's the food you promised us?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Oh! Yes, about that. I have some complimentary packets of Equal and a half-empty jar of garlic powder. You're welcome.

dialogue.speaker: City Council Member
dialogue.line: That's not food.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: It's a condiment situation. Work with me here. Meeting adjourned!

jokes.text: Bees! Bees! Oh! Nobody panic! Ohhh, noooo! Ah! It stung me in the eyeball!
jokes.setup: Wall breaking ceremony proceeding
jokes.punchline: Chaos erupting as hidden bees attack everyone
jokes.explanation: Good physical comedy escalation, high impact but not particularly witty

dialogue.speaker: Leslie Knope
dialogue.line: Everyone, please remain calm. The wall breaking ceremony is proceeding exactly as planned.

dialogue.speaker: Ben Wyatt
dialogue.line: Leslie, there are bees everywhere!

dialogue.speaker: Leslie Knope
dialogue.line: Those weren't on the schedule.

dialogue.speaker: Ron Swanson
dialogue.line: I specifically requested a bee-free zone.

dialogue.speaker: April Ludgate
dialogue.line: This is the best day ever.

dialogue.speaker: Tom Haverford
dialogue.line: Treat yo self... to a bee sting! No, that doesn't work!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry Gergich
dialogue.line: I'm allergic! Why is this happening to me?

jokes.text: The stupid Eagletonians are totally getting the pwned by these bees. I'm gonna send this straight to tosh.
jokes.setup: Bee attack chaos
jokes.punchline: Resident more interested in viral video opportunity than helping
jokes.explanation: Captures social media age priorities but uses dated internet speak

dialogue.speaker: Pawnee Resident
dialogue.line: Wait, wait, wait! Someone get this on video! This is going to be huge on TikTok!

jokes.text: Don't worry, babe, I'll protect you. I got stung once, I'm immune. Go ahead and sting me, bees. It does nothing.
jokes.setup: Man trying to protect girlfriend from bees
jokes.punchline: Completely wrong understanding of bee sting immunity
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist logic about bee immunity, but character isn't well-established

dialogue.speaker: Man
dialogue.line: Don't worry, I've been stung by bees before. I'm immune now.

dialogue.speaker: Girlfriend
dialogue.line: That's not how immunity works.

dialogue.speaker: Man
dialogue.line: Sure it is. I got stung last summer, and I didn't die. So now my body knows how to handle it.

dialogue.speaker: Girlfriend
dialogue.line: You're going to get us both killed.

jokes.text: Plus, one Pawneean was stung... in his mouth because he was laughing at the Eagletonians.
jokes.setup: Leslie explaining that mostly Eagletonians got stung
jokes.punchline: Revealing poetic justice for the one Pawnee victim
jokes.explanation: Nice ironic justice - the one Pawnee victim got stung for being cruel

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: The one Pawnee resident who got stung? Ben Wyatt. Poetic justice.

jokes.text: Nice try, prank queen. Probably bees in there.
jokes.setup: Leslie denying bee prank accusations
jokes.punchline: Reporter assuming she has bees everywhere
jokes.explanation: Shows how conspiracy theories spiral - now every wall is suspect

dialogue.speaker: Reporter
dialogue.line: So you're saying you had nothing to do with the bee incident?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie Knope
dialogue.line: That's right. I would never prank anyone with bees.

dialogue.speaker: Reporter
dialogue.line: But you do have bees? Like, a lot of bees?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie Knope
dialogue.line: What? No! Why would I have bees?

dialogue.speaker: Reporter
dialogue.line: So the bees everywhere are just a coincidence?

jokes.text: I am trying to fix my bee hole disaster. Wait! No, wait! No! Larry, don't tell him that. Don't mention my bee hole.
jokes.setup: Leslie trying to reschedule important meeting
jokes.punchline: Accidentally creating sexual innuendo with bee hole reference
jokes.explanation: Good accidental innuendo humor, though relying on cheap sexual reference

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: We need to reschedule the meeting. How about Thursday at 2?

dialogue.speaker: Parks Department Employee
dialogue.line: That doesn't work. I have to inspect the bee hole.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: The bee hole? We could do it in the bee hole.

dialogue.speaker: Parks Department Employee
dialogue.line: Um... okay.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I mean, if you're already going to be in the bee hole, we might as well have the meeting there. In the bee hole.

jokes.text: My son is several weeks old. He's quite familiar with the sound of power tools.
jokes.setup: Construction crew warning about noise
jokes.punchline: Ron's matter-of-fact response about exposing baby to construction
jokes.explanation: Good Ron parenting philosophy - treating baby like tiny construction worker

dialogue.speaker: Ron Swanson
dialogue.line: I'm not worried. My son was exposed to construction noise in the womb. It toughened him up.

jokes.text: Lasik for fingernails? Cut your fingernails once, never have to cut 'em again. All we need is a complicated laser I have no idea how to build.
jokes.setup: Tom pitching business ideas
jokes.punchline: Completely impractical application of medical technology
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist business idea - misunderstanding of how lasik works applied to vanity

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Okay, okay, okay. So I'm thinking we take those fancy MRI machines, right? The ones that cost like two million dollars?

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: And we turn them into tanning beds. High-tech tanning beds. We call it 'MRI Tan.'

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: People want to look good AND know what their spleen looks like. It's a win-win.

jokes.text: 'Saltweens!' Saltines for tweens.
jokes.setup: Tom continuing business pitch
jokes.punchline: Unnecessary demographic targeting of crackers
jokes.explanation: Good portmanteau and absurd market segmentation typical of Tom

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: We're targeting an underserved demographic: people who eat crackers. It's a $47 billion industry.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Tom, that's just... everyone eats crackers.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Exactly. We're being very specific about our market.

jokes.text: Restaurants were ranked number one in Accounting Magazine's 'Riskiest Businesses' issue. That was their last issue before they folded, actually. Magazines are also very risky.
jokes.setup: Ben citing magazine about business risks
jokes.punchline: Revealing the magazine itself failed, extending the irony
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalating irony - magazine about business risk went out of business, perfectly delivered

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: According to this article in Business Monthly, the top business risks are overexpansion, poor management, and—

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Oh no. Business Monthly just went out of business.

jokes.text: You're 52 years old! No, I'm not.
jokes.setup: Ben saying Tom should have business ideas by now
jokes.punchline: Tom's immediate age denial
jokes.explanation: Quick character moment about Tom's vanity, but fairly predictable

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: You should have some solid business ideas by now.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: I'm not that old!

jokes.text: You would be the middleman between dry cleaners and the companies that sell chemicals... to dry cleaners. Wow! Right? You're right, man. That is not sexy.
jokes.setup: Ben excitedly explaining dry cleaning chemical business
jokes.punchline: Tom's underwhelmed response and Ben's disappointed agreement
jokes.explanation: Good character contrast - Ben's excitement for boring business vs Tom's need for glamour

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Tom, I've been thinking about getting into the dry cleaning chemical business. It's actually a really underrated industry with huge profit margins.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: That sounds... great, man.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: You don't sound excited.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: No, I am. Dry cleaning chemicals. That's... that's the dream.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Yeah, okay. You're right. It's not that exciting.

jokes.text: When he sinks his teeth into something, he's like a dog with a bone! Oh, my God! You can't lean in to whisper and then yell. That's not fair.
jokes.setup: Leslie whispering about reporter then getting excited
jokes.punchline: April critiquing Leslie's inconsistent volume
jokes.explanation: Good meta-humor about Leslie's inability to control her enthusiasm level

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I can't believe that reporter is here. This is huge for Pawnee.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: You just whispered that and now you're yelling.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I'm excited!

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Your volume control is like a broken dimmer switch.

jokes.text: Aghh! I can't handle all this fighting. It's like Thanksgiving 2004 all over again! Don't even ask!
jokes.setup: Craig overwhelmed by conflict
jokes.punchline: Mysterious traumatic Thanksgiving reference
jokes.explanation: Good Craig character moment - his dramatic reactions and mysterious past trauma

dialogue.speaker: Craig
dialogue.line: I don't do well with conflict. Last Thanksgiving, I had to choose between my family and my principles, and let's just say the cops sided with my family.

jokes.text: I'm sure the government believed you when you told them that, and I tip my cap to you for profiting off their ignoe. But I'm offering you two months paid vacation and the ability to take credit for my work, which will be flawless.
jokes.setup: Construction crew saying job takes 6-8 weeks
jokes.punchline: Ron offering to do their work while they relax and take credit
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ron moment - his craftsman pride and anti-government sentiment in ideal solution

dialogue.speaker: Construction Foreman
dialogue.line: This job is going to take six to eight weeks.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Why don't you fellas take a break? Relax. I'll do the work.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: You can take all the credit.

jokes.text: We buy tetrachloroethylene at $1.60 a gallon, but we sell it back at 2.38 a gallon. This is so boring! This is like listening to a TED talk by the color beige.
jokes.setup: Ben excitedly explaining chemical pricing
jokes.punchline: Tom's creative metaphor for extreme boredom
jokes.explanation: 'TED talk by the color beige' is an excellent and quotable metaphor for boring content

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Did you know that phosphate pricing has increased 340% in the last five years?

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Yeah, I did know that. Because you told me. While I was being slowly buried alive in a box of dirt.

jokes.text: What do you got in there for me, some dirty mags? No! These gift baskets are for innocent victims... not for jerks who got stung because they were laughing at other people's pain.
jokes.setup: Jeremy asking about Leslie's hospital visit gifts
jokes.punchline: Leslie excluding him from sympathy for being cruel
jokes.explanation: Good moral distinction and Leslie's attempt to maintain ethical standards

dialogue.speaker: Jeremy
dialogue.line: Did you get a lot of gifts when you were in the hospital?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Oh, I got flowers, balloons, a casserole, get-well cards from half the department.

dialogue.speaker: Jeremy
dialogue.line: That's nice.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: You know what you're not getting? Sympathy. Because you've been really cruel to me.

jokes.text: We're live ambushing Leslie Knope who's in the middle of a crass publicity stunt, shoving cameras cameras into the faces of these poor, injured victims.
jokes.setup: Reporter describing Leslie's hospital visit
jokes.punchline: Accusing her of exploiting victims while literally exploiting victims himself
jokes.explanation: Good ironic hypocrisy - reporter doing exactly what he's accusing Leslie of

dialogue.speaker: Mike Patterson
dialogue.line: Leslie Knope showed up at Ramsett Hospital today, ostensibly to visit an injured constituent. But was she really there to help? Or was she there to exploit this tragedy for her own political gain?

dialogue.speaker: Mike Patterson
dialogue.line: We'll have more on this story as it develops.

jokes.text: It's to distract people from my black eye. See? It's working.
jokes.setup: April asking about Leslie's tie-dye shirt
jokes.punchline: Leslie's backwards logic about distraction technique
jokes.explanation: Shows Leslie's poor crisis management and flawed logic

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Why are you wearing a tie-dye shirt?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I'm using a distraction technique. While everyone's looking at my shirt, I can get work done.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: That's not how that works.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Well, it's working right now because you're distracted by my shirt.

jokes.text: I just wanted you to have a brief moment of happiness before you got sadder.
jokes.setup: April claiming there was good news
jokes.punchline: Revealing she was just giving false hope before more bad news
jokes.explanation: Perfect April deadpan - her twisted version of being caring

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: I have some good news.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Just kidding. I wanted to give you hope before I crushed it with bad news.

jokes.text: It's just like a big, fun... stinking turd nugget. It sucks.
jokes.setup: Leslie trying to stay positive about challenges
jokes.punchline: Her optimistic metaphor collapsing into crude reality
jokes.explanation: Good collapse of Leslie's forced optimism into honest frustration

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: You know, I think of challenges like clouds. They're big and scary, but the sun is always behind them, and eventually they drift away.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: That's actually just a cloud of bird poop that fell on my windshield this morning.

jokes.text: Ben! You brought the wrong easel. God! Can't take this guy anywhere.
jokes.setup: Tom's restaurant presentation has wrong materials
jokes.punchline: Tom blaming Ben while asking for his help
jokes.explanation: Shows Tom's inability to take responsibility even when asking for help

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Ben! BEN! Why would you give me the wrong materials for my presentation?

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: I didn't give you anything, Tom. You asked for those yourself.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Well, can you help me fix this?

jokes.text: Liam Bonneville actually used it to humiliate staffers, but... he's kind of a bad boy.
jokes.setup: Grant explaining how Leslie's report was misused
jokes.punchline: Treating bureaucratic cruelty as roguish charm
jokes.explanation: Good callback and absurd government 'bad boy' concept

jokes.text: Sunday brunch with scrambled eggs so fluffy they'll be like little pillows for your bacon. Bacon pillows. I like the sound of that.
jokes.setup: Tom describing restaurant's brunch concept
jokes.punchline: Creating appealing food metaphor that investor immediately adopts
jokes.explanation: Good food metaphor that works for Tom's style and appeals to investor

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: We're gonna serve brunch items, but we're gonna call them 'breakfast for dinner.' No wait, that's already a thing. Okay, how about 'brinner'?

dialogue.speaker: Mitch
dialogue.line: I like that. Brinner.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Right? It's like breakfast had a baby with dinner, and that baby is brinner.

dialogue.speaker: Mitch
dialogue.line: That's genius. I'm in. Let's do brinner.

jokes.text: Hey, kid... your boss here sure knows his stuff.
jokes.setup: Ben has been providing all the actual business knowledge
jokes.punchline: Mitch crediting Tom when Ben did all the real work
jokes.explanation: Decent ironic moment but fairly predictable workplace comedy situation

dialogue.speaker: Mitch
dialogue.line: I have to give all the credit to Tom. He really knows his stuff when it comes to business.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Wait, what? I did all the work on this project.

dialogue.speaker: Mitch
dialogue.line: Right, but Tom was there too.

jokes.text: Yesterday, I fixed that old steam radiator. Threaded a new valve into the side, removed the handle from the base, unscrewed the packing nut from the stem. This subpar toolbox only had one wrench, so I was lucky it was a three-quarters or I'd have been screwed.
jokes.setup: Ron responding to Leslie's existential crisis
jokes.punchline: Launching into detailed technical repair story instead of advice
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ron misdirection - avoiding emotional conversation with technical details

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Leslie, I'm going to tell you something I wish someone had told me when I was your age. Back in 1987, I was working on a Craftsman table saw. The blade kept binding up on me, no matter what I did. I adjusted the fence, checked the alignment, replaced the belt. Nothing worked. So I sat down, and I thought about that saw for three days straight. By the fourth day, I realized the problem wasn't the saw. It was me. I wasn't respecting the saw. Once I started respecting the saw, everything got better.

jokes.text: You like fixing this town, Leslie, you always have. You know it's an uphill battle, but you love the struggle.
jokes.setup: Ron's long radiator story seeming irrelevant
jokes.punchline: Revealing it was actually perfect metaphor for Leslie's situation
jokes.explanation: Excellent delayed payoff - Ron's seemingly random story becomes perfect metaphorical advice, showing his wisdom

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: And that's why you need to let it cool down before you touch it again.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Ron, what does any of that have to do with my situation?

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Everything, Leslie. You're the radiator.

jokes.text: You like fixing this town, Leslie, you always have. You know it's an uphill battle, but you love the struggle.
jokes.setup: Ron's radiator metaphor seeming like avoidance
jokes.punchline: The revelation that it was actually perfect advice disguised as mechanical talk
jokes.explanation: Excellent payoff - Ron's mechanical metaphor becomes profound life advice, perfectly character-integrated

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: You know, a radiator is like a relationship. If it's not functioning properly, you need to get under there and figure out what's wrong with it. You can't just ignore it and hope it fixes itself.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Ron, are you seriously giving me relationship advice right now?

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I'm talking about radiators.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: That's exactly what I mean. You're avoiding—

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: But if you actually listen to what I said, it applies to everything. Including your situation with Ben.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Oh my God. That was... that was actually really good advice.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I know. I'm very good at advice. I just prefer to deliver it through the metaphor of home maintenance.

jokes.text: What's that? Nothing. Clearing my throat.
jokes.setup: Emotional moment after Ron's advice
jokes.punchline: Ron denying any emotional response
jokes.explanation: Classic Ron emotional avoidance - refusing to admit he was moved by the moment

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I'm not crying. There's just a lot of smoke in here.

jokes.text: Okay, wow, that's very misleading phraseology.
jokes.setup: Leslie saying she can't think of one reason not to take job
jokes.punchline: Grant's bureaucratic response to her confusing double negative
jokes.explanation: Good bureaucratic response to Leslie's confusing way of speaking

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I can't think of one reason not to take this job.

dialogue.speaker: Grant
dialogue.line: Well, technically, a double negative is a positive, so you've actually given me one reason to hire you.

jokes.text: That's not your hand. Here it is. Sorry, my depth perception is totally screwed up.
jokes.setup: Leslie trying to shake hands after meeting
jokes.punchline: Missing because of her black eye affecting vision
jokes.explanation: Physical callback to bee attack consequences, but fairly predictable

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I'm so glad to meet you, Mr. Grant. I'd love to shake your hand.

dialogue.speaker: Grant
dialogue.line: Of course.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Oh no, I can't quite... my depth perception is off today.

jokes.text: I know this is a symbolic gesture, but my investor floor is $1,000. So as of now, you own zero shares.
jokes.setup: Ben offering to invest in Tom's restaurant
jokes.punchline: Tom immediately clarifying Ben's gesture means nothing financially
jokes.explanation: Good Tom character moment - his business focus overriding friendship gestures

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: I want to invest in your restaurant.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Wait, what? Really?

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Yeah, I believe in you.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Oh my God, Ben, that's... wait. How much are we talking?

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Well, I was thinking—

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Never mind. Doesn't matter. I don't want your money.

jokes.text: Most people in this world, John, are ass[bleep]. This is the best day I've ever spent in a government building, and luckily my son was here to share it with me.
jokes.setup: Ron reflecting on his day with baby
jokes.punchline: Teaching cynical life lessons to newborn while having wholesome father-son time
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ron parenting - combining deep cynicism with genuine father-son bonding

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I spent the day teaching my son about the government's fundamental incompetence and the dangers of trusting bureaucrats. It was beautiful.

jokes.text: He now has that satisfied look that only comes with the pride of labor. Or he pooped. Either way, well done, John.
jokes.setup: Ron attributing baby's expression to work satisfaction
jokes.punchline: Acknowledging more likely biological explanation while maintaining pride
jokes.explanation: Excellent structure and character work - Ron projecting his values while acknowledging baby reality

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: He's got that look of a man who just closed a deal with the Parks Department.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Ron, he's three months old. He probably just has gas.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: That's what I said. He closed a deal.

tmdb_overview: Leslie aims to create a new town slogan, but runs afoul of local shock jocks. Andy scouts bands to play the Unity Concert and discovers an interesting secret about Ron, while Tom looks for a restaurant locale with Donna and April.
insight: Seventy-two laughs on absurdist character work, but emotional stakes soften the comedic edge.
